perturbation.original_text: No later than thirty (30) days following the Closing, each of AWP and the Company shall change its respective corporate name and trade name and cause its organizational documents to be amended to remove any reference to "Armstrong."
perturbation.changed_text: No later than ten (10) days following the Closing, each of AWP and the Company shall change its respective corporate name and trade name and cause its organizational documents to be amended to remove any reference to "Armstrong."
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the period for changing the corporate name to 10 days might be unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with statutory requirements for corporate name changes in Delaware, where the entities are incorporated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law, Title 8, Section 242
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires specific procedures for amending a certificate of incorporation, which can take longer than 10 days, particularly if shareholder approval is needed. Reducing the timeframe to 10 days might not allow sufficient time to comply with these mandatory legal procedures, creating a contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 251 Â§ 252 Â§ 253 Â§ 254 Â§ 255 Â§ 256 Â§ 257 Â§ 258 Â§ 259 Â§ 260 Â§ 261 Â§ 262 Â§ 263 Â§ 264 Â§ 265 Â§ 266 Â§ 267 Â§ 268 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IX. Merger, Consolidation or Conversion Â§ 251. Merger or consolidation of domestic corporations [For application of this section, see 79 Del. Laws, c. 327, Â§ 8; 80 Del. Laws, c. 265, Â§ 17; and 82 Del. Laws, c. 256, Â§ 24]. (a) Any 2 or more corporations of this State may merge into a single surviving corporation, which may be any 1 of the constituent corporations or may consolidate into a new resulting corporation formed by the consolidation, pursuant to an agreement of merger or consolidation, as the case may be, complying and approved in accordance with this section. (b) The board of directors of each corporation which desires to merge or consolidate shall adopt a resolution approving an agreement of merger or consolidation and declaring its advisability. The agreement shall state: (1) The terms and conditions of the merger or consolidation; (2) The mode of carrying the same into effect; (3) In the case of a merger, such amendments or changes in the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation as are desired to be effected by the merger (which amendments or changes may amend and restate the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation in its entirety), or, if no such amendments or changes are desired, a statement that the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation shall be its certificate of incorporation; (4) In the case of a consolidation, that the certificate of incorporation of the resulting corporation shall be as is set forth in an attachment to the agreement; (5) The manner, if any, of converting the shares of each of the constituent corporations into shares or other securities of the corporation surviving or resulting from the merger or consolidation, or of cancelling some or all of such share
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time to change corporate names from 30 to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law requires specific procedures for amending a certificate of incorporation, which can take longer than 10 days, especially if shareholder approval is needed. The scraped law snippet 1, Title 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law, suggests that mergers and consolidations require board of director resolutions and agreement, which all take time. Thus, the reduced timeframe potentially contradicts these procedural requirements by not allowing sufficient time for compliance, creating an inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to remedy such breach within thirty (30) days' written notice thereof; provided, however, that if the material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party is limited to the Licensed Copyrights, Licensed Know-How, the Arizona Licensed Patents, or the Licensed Trademarks, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to termination solely with respect to the affected part of the license (i.e., in such case, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Copyrights or the Licensed Know-How or the Arizona Licensed Patents or the Licensed Trademarks, as applicable).
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to remedy such breach within ten (10) days' written notice thereof; provided, however, that if the material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party is limited to the Licensed Copyrights, Licensed Know-How, the Arizona Licensed Patents, or the Licensed Trademarks, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to termination solely with respect to the affected part of the license (i.e., in such case, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Copyrights or the Licensed Know-How or the Arizona Licensed Patents or the Licensed Trademarks, as applicable).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable, particularly if complex issues need to be resolved. This might conflict with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware contract law, which requires a reasonable opportunity to cure breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Delaware)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Reducing the cure period to 10 days may be seen as failing to provide a reasonable opportunity to cure a material breach, potentially violating this implied covenant, especially if complex actions are required to remedy the breach.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for a material breach from 30 to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, and reducing the cure period to 10 days may violate this implied covenant by not providing a reasonable opportunity to cure a material breach. Even though there are no scraped law snippets, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both point to the unreasonableness of the shortened cure period and a potential violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. This directly contradicts the implied legal requirement to provide a reasonable cure period.
